data.title: Congressional budget process failures defy big fix
data.description:
You know something climactic is about to occur in Congress when even senior legislators begin to toss around sports metaphors like “a hail Mary pass” or “a quarterback sneak.”

Last week, 11-term Rep. Virginia Foxx (R-N.C.), chair of the House Rules Committee, at a meeting on granting special rules for six remaining appropriations bills, observed that the measures were the product of bipartisan and bicameral agreements.

“With this final tranche of appropriations bills coming before the committee,” she said, “the House stands on the one-yard line — we’re primed to complete consideration of full year [fiscal 2026] funding across all 12 bills.”

But even when poised to score from the one-yard line, things can go wrong. An interception or fumble can turn the whole game around.

That’s what almost happened last week when House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) announced that his Democratic colleagues would oppose the Homeland Security funding bill, which includes funding for ICE. Jeffries said changes must be made to rein in immigration enforcement and the violent tactics that have resulted in two recent killings of U.S. citizens in Minneapolis. The Senate was not far behind, as Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) threatened to take the whole package of remaining bills down if the ICE component was either not stripped or not significantly altered.

The special House rule that made in order both the three-bill package for the departments of Defense, Labor-HHS, and Transportation, and the separate bill for Homeland Security, narrowly passed the House last Thursday, 214-213. The Homeland Security bill subsequently passed, 220 to 207, with seven Democrats breaking ranks. The three-bill package passed by a more comfortable vote of 341-88.

All four measures were bundled into a mini-bus bill by the rule along with two other appropriations bills previously passed by the House for financial services and the Department of State before being sent to the Senate where debate began Tuesday. The Senate outcome was too close to call at this writing, as even the Democratic dealmakers of the bipartisan-bicameral compromise withdrew their support unless tough new restrictions are imposed on ICE.

That approach did not sit well with Republican leaders, who thought the compromise deal had been sealed. If the votes are no longer there and further alterations in ICE’s authority and tactics are not struck, another government shutdown may be imminent at midnight tomorrow — the deadline imposed in last year’s compromise that ended the record-breaking 43-day government shutdown.

The delay in the government funding process is nothing new. According to “Vital Statistics on Congress,” in fiscal years 1977 through 2019, Congress enacted all 13 (now 12) regular appropriations bills only three times before the start of the fiscal year — all in the last century. It has enacted none of the regular bills before Oct. 1 on 15 occasions, 12 of those in this century.

To call the budget process broken is an understatement. Mega process reforms have a tendency to become top-heavy with complex demands and eventually fail. The big reforms of the 1970s in the wake of Vietnam, Watergate and the imperial presidency, whether on war powers, intelligence oversight, or budgetary transparency and fiscal discipline, have all come a cropper. I call it the Humpty Dumpty phenomenon: they are all propped atop a grand wall, built on high hopes, and eventually destined to suffer a great fall.

Can the budget process be fixed? Probably not. The history of congressional reform efforts is littered with the carcasses of overly ambitious efforts designed to address every imaginable defect. This year’s fiscal foibles at budgeting track the recent historical pattern of eroding discipline and lax enforcement as Congress becomes more bitterly partisan. Further process reforms are the last thing members are seeking. Instead, they are more intent on launching partisan messaging campaigns aimed at picking up more seats at the next election. The sports metaphors seem less farfetched as politics becomes a blood sport.

Ironically, it may take divided party government to bring Congress back to its intended purpose of making all laws necessary and proper to promote the general welfare. Divided government can lead to more gridlock, but it can also force members to recognize that their political fortunes depend on working together to find common ground, especially in times of national crises and flagging public trust in government.

There is no sports analogy for opposing teams joining forces to accommodate most fans in the stands most of the time. No flag can be thrown to incentivize sportsmanlike conduct.

Don Wolfensberger is a 28-year congressional staff veteran culminating as chief of staff for the House Rules Committee in 1995. He is author of, “Congress and the People: Deliberative Democracy on Trial” (2000), and, “Changing Cultures in Congress: From Fair Play to Power Plays” (2018).

data.title: 'National Shutdown' set for Friday: What to know
data.description:
Video above: Government funding deadline looms as Democrats oppose ICE actions

(NEXSTAR) – In response to recent U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) activity, as well as the deaths of four people shot by federal agents, activists are encouraging a “shutdown” on Friday.

The “National Shutdown” calls for supporters to stay home from work and school, and not to go shopping in an effort to “stop funding ICE.”

“The people of the Twin Cities have shown the way for the whole country — to stop ICE’s reign of terror, we need to SHUT IT DOWN. On Friday, January 30, join a nationwide day of no school, no work and no shopping,” organizers wrote online.

Last Friday, businesses across Minnesota shuttered in protest of ICE being in the Minneapolis area. A day later, Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care unit nurse, was fatally shot by federal agents.

Organizers of Friday’s National Shutdown also pointed to the shooting deaths of Renee Good, Silverio Villegas González, and Keith Porter Jr., saying “they were gunned down in broad daylight simply for exercising their First Amendment right to protest mass deportation.”

“It is time for us to all stand up together in a nationwide shutdown and say enough is enough!”

Several University of Minnesota-based organizations, including the Somali Student Association, the Ethiopian Student Association, the Black Student Union, the Liberian Student Association, and the Graduate Labor Union, have endorsed Friday’s National Shutdown.

The political organization 50501 (50 States, 50 Protests, 1 Day), which has been at the center of several national protests since President Trump took office, is also endorsing the event. (50501, too, is supporting “ICE Out of Everywhere” events, hosted by Action Network, which are scheduled for Saturday.)

The website for Friday’s National Shutdown lists hundreds of other supporting organizations across the U.S.

Actors Pedro Pascal and Hannah Einbinder, as well as rapper Macklemore are among those who have promoted the general strike in separate Instagram posts.

“No system built on violence has ever been undone by reform,” the latter wrote. “It ends when people stop sustaining it. This is that moment.”

As they were during Minnesota’s statewide strike, businesses are being encouraged to close on Friday in solidarity. Several establishments in Denver are doing just that, Nexstar’s KDVR reported Thursday.

“It has been increasingly difficult to watch what is unfolding in our country,” a representative for award-winning Denver restaurant Sắp Sửa said in a post announcing its one-day closure. “We have felt so helpless and alone and it’s abundantly clear that no one will come to save us, so it is our civic duty to unite as a community in support of the most vulnerable.”

New ‘No Kings’ protests scheduled

Earlier this week, organizers announced a third round of “No Kings” protests coming this spring, to oppose what they describe as authoritarianism under Trump.

Previous rallies have drawn millions of people, and organizers said they expect even greater numbers on March 28 in the wake of Trump’s immigration crackdown in Minneapolis.

“We expect this to be the largest protest in American history,” Ezra Levin, co-executive director of the nonprofit Indivisible, told The Associated Press ahead of Wednesday’s announcement. He predicted that as many as 9 million people will turn out.

“No Kings” protests, which are organized by a constellation of groups around the country, have been a focal point for outrage over Trump’s attempts to consolidate and expand his power.

ICE operations winding down in Minnesota, Maine

Meanwhile, Trump’s border czar, Tom Homan, said Thursday that the administration is open to a drawdown of agents in Minneapolis but only after he sees cooperation from state officials.

And in Maine, Sen. Susan Collins said that immigration officials have ceased their “enhanced operations” in the state after more than 100 arrests in an enforcement surge. She made the announcement after saying she had several direct communications with Noem.

“There are currently no ongoing or planned large-scale ICE operations here,” Collins said in a statement. “I have been urging Secretary Noem and others in the Administration to get ICE to reconsider its approach to immigration enforcement in the state.”

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: Live updates: Senate vote pushes government closer to shutdown; Noem mum at Trump Cabinet meeting
data.description:
President Trump assembled his Cabinet for a meeting on Thursday, touting his administration’s achievements after a year in office. He made some news in saying he would announce his nominee for Federal Reserve chair next week.

But in a break from tradition, not every Cabinet member spoke. Among those sitting and listening was Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, who has drawn immense scrutiny after two deaths at the hands of federal immigration officers in recent weeks.

Meanwhile, on Capitol Hill, Senate Democrats and a few Republicans took down a procedural vote that would have advanced a major government funding package. If a deal isn’t made by 11:59 p.m. Friday, part of the government will shut down.

Trump administration border czar Tom Homan said at a press conference Thursday morning that the immigration crackdown in Minneapolis will be done “by the book” as President Trump looks to “fix” it.

“I do not want to hear that everything that has been done here has been perfect. Nothing is ever perfect. Anything can be improved on. What we’ve been working on is making this operation safer, more efficient, by the book,” Homan said Thursday.

After Saturday’s fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by immigration officials in Minneapolis, the fight over President Trump’s immigration enforcement operations has reached a fever pitch across the country and on Capitol Hill. Some Senate Republicans, however, have said they are open to splitting off the DHS funding from a six-bill package to avoid another shutdown.

Trump later Thursday will join first lady Melania Trump at the premiere of her documentary at the Kennedy Center.

Follow live below.

data.title: Shapiro: Vance 'has offered comfort' to 'antisemites on the right'
data.description:
Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) slammed Vice President Vance in a Wednesday interview for making statements that “comfort” antisemites on the right, after Vance posted about Holocaust Remembrance Day without explicitly mentioning Jews. 

“Remember that the reason why we memorialize the Holocaust on this day, really, essentially, is to never forget,” Shapiro, who is Jewish, told NBC News. 

“And the reason you want to never forget is so that we never live through that atrocity again. Part of never forgetting is making sure that the facts of what happened are recited, are remembered. The fact that JD Vance couldn’t bring himself to acknowledging that 6 million Jews were killed by Hitler and by the Nazis speaks volumes,” he added.

Vance in his post had reflected on the millions killed during the Holocaust.

“Today we remember the millions of lives lost during the Holocaust, the millions of stories of individual bravery and heroism, and one of the enduring lessons of one of the darkest chapters in human history: that while humans create beautiful things and are full of compassion, we’re also capable of unspeakable brutality,” the vice president wrote in a post on X.

“And we promise never again to go down the darkest path,” he added.

Shapiro also said Vance’s past statements show that he “openly embraces neo-Nazis and neo-Nazi political parties, given the way in which he has offered comfort, really, to the antisemites on the right who are infecting the Republican Party.”

Shapiro cited Vance’s support for Germany’s far-right AfD party, saying it signaled a broader lack of concerns for Jewish people.

The Pennsylvania governor said, “it’s not a shock to me that he would omit” Jewish people from his post recognizing Holocaust Remembrance Day, “but it’s a sad day that the vice president of the United States on Holocaust Awareness Day couldn’t address that.”

The White House quickly fired back to Shapiro’s remarks in a statement to NBC News, telling the outlet his words represent “next level hypocritical deflection from Shapiro, a misguided plea for attention from a political lightweight.” 

Officials said Shapiro’s own tweet did not mention violence against Jews.

“This is an insane standard and a BS attack,” former Trump campaign staffer Alex Brusewitz wrote in a post on X. 

“The Vice President literally posted a photo of himself [and] the Second Lady at Dachau. He has been an incredible friend to both the Jewish community and Israel.”

data.title: Trump says he asked Putin to stop bombing Kyiv for one week
data.description:
President Trump said on Thursday that he “personally asked” Russian President Vladmir Putin to stop bombing Ukraine’s capital for one week as the country grapples with bitterly cold temperatures.  

“I personally asked President Putin not to fire on Kyiv and the cities and town for a week during this extraordinary…it’s not just like cold…it’s extraordinary cold, record-setting cold over there too,” Trump said during his monthly Cabinet meeting at the White House.

Trump said Putin agreed to his request and that Ukraine was “very happy about it.”

“I have to tell you, it was very nice. A lot of people said, ‘Don’t waste the call. You’re not going to get that.’ And he did it, and we’re very happy that they did it because on top of everything else, that’s not what they need is missiles coming into their towns and cities,” Trump added.

Temperatures reaching as low as minus 30 degrees Celsius, or minus 22 degree Fahrenheit, are expected to hit Ukraine next week, bringing a frost lasting several days, according to Reuters.

Trump’s remarks came as U.S. Special Envoy to the Middle East Steve Witkoff was discussing the state of negotiations between the U.S. and Russia as it relates to the war in Ukraine.

Witkoff noted that he and the president’s son-in-law Jared Kushner made “a lot of progress” during a meeting with five Russian generals in the United Arab Emirates last Sunday.

“The talks will continue in about a week, but lots of good things happening between counterparts,” he said. “Discussing the land deal, we have a security protocol agreement that’s largely finished, a prosperity agreement that’s largely finished.”

“And I think the people of Ukraine are now hopeful and expect that we’re going to deliver a peace deal sometime soon,” Witkoff added.

data.title: Fulton County chairman: 'We have nothing, nothing, nothing to hide'
data.description:
Fulton County, Ga., Chairman Robb Pitts on Thursday said county election officials have nothing to hide after the FBI retrieved boxes of 2020 election data, including ballots, during the execution of a search warrant.

“We in Fulton County have nothing, nothing, nothing to hide,” Pitts said at a press conference in Atlanta. “And finally, and most importantly, this is by no means over. This is by no means over. And we‘re grateful for everyone around the country who is in this fight with us. And they are with us from every corner of this great nation. Any honest review of these files will show what every, every previous review has shown.”

“Fulton County elections are fair and lawful, and the outcome of the 2020 election will not change,” he added. “Period.”

Pitts and Fulton County Elections Board Chair Sherri Allen said FBI agents took 700 boxes of ballots among the materials collected between 12 and 9 p.m. EST on Wednesday. They said that Fulton County was scheduled to discuss how best to deliver the election records. The records, as the subject of an active litigation, “were likely to be unsealed and turned over in a matter of weeks,” Pitts said.

“Our attorneys carefully reviewed the search warrant and advised me and others that it was in the best interest of Fulton County to comply, which we did,” the chairman told reporters.

Pitts said county officials do not know where the election data is now being held or by whom, and emphasized that Fulton County “can no longer be held responsible for those ballots and other data that was seized yesterday.”

He said Fulton County was still a “target” for potential harassment ahead of the 2026 midterm elections in November, adding that he’s also a target.

“I’m a big boy,” the chairman said. “Now, while we‘ve grown accustomed to name-calling and rhetoric, we will not give one inch to those who seek to take control of our elections.”

He reflected on 2020 and said he told President Trump and former Reps. Matt Gaetz (R-Fla.) and Marjorie Taylor Green (R-Ga.) to “stay the hell out of Fulton County.”

Pitts and Allen did not reveal what the county’s next steps are. Pitts said he was “confident, confident” that the documents in the boxes were secure while in the county’s possession.

Agents executed “a court authorized law enforcement action” at the county’s main office in Union City on Wednesday, according to an FBI statement obtained by NewsNation, The Hill’s cable partner. The investigation remains ongoing.

Also present at the elections office during the search warrant’s execution was Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard and FBI Deputy Director Andrew Bailey.

Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) questioned Gabbard’s presence and accused her of “once again demonstrating her utter lack of fitness for the office that she holds by injecting the nonpartisan intelligence community she is supposed to be leading into a domestic political stunt designed to legitimize conspiracy theories that undermine our democracy.”

“Either is a serious breach of trust that further underscores why she is totally unqualified to hold a position that demands sound judgment, apolitical independence, and a singular focus on keeping Americans safe,” Warner wrote on the social platform X.

Fulton County became the focal point in Trump’s criminal case brought by District Attorney Fani Willis in 2023. She accused Trump of conspiring to subvert the election results and win Georgia to stay in office. The case was dropped in 2025 before Trump returned to the White House and after Willis was disqualified from the case.

data.title: Watch live: Frey speaks in DC as Minneapolis reels from Trump immigration enforcement
data.description:
Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) will give remarks Thursday at the winter meeting of the U.S. Conference of Mayors in Washington, D.C., as his home state reels in the wake of two fatal shootings at the hands of immigration enforcement officials.

Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem, who has defended the officers involved in the deaths, is facing mounting criticism over the agency’s enforcement tactics in Minnesota. Many Democratic lawmakers and local officials have urged President Trump to oust Noem in recent days.

Frey, who met with Tom Homan earlier this week after the border czar was sent to Minneapolis, has doubled down on his sanctuary city policies. He told Homan that the city would not help with the Trump administration’s immigration operations.

The mayor’s speech is scheduled to begin at 1:45 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: America does not have a protein deficiency problem: It has a fiber problem
data.description:
In 2025, the Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee, an independent panel of scientists, published a report that recommended shifting protein intake away from red and processed meats and toward beans, peas, lentils, other vegetable protein sources and seafood. They relied on robust research showing that fiber-rich plant-forward diets are associated with better health.

Earlier this month, however, the U.S. Department of Agriculture and the Department of Health and Human Services rejected that recommendation and released new dietary guidelines that urge Americans to eat more meat and dairy.

At a minimum, federal nutrition policy should clearly explain when and why it departs from the conclusions of its own scientific advisory committee, particularly when those decisions shape national food programs and public understanding of health.

When an administration dismisses these conclusions as not “evidence-based” without clear justification, it raises questions about how scientific advice is weighed in federal policymaking. Because the dietary guidelines shape school meals, military rations and federal nutrition assistance programs, sidelining evidence weakens the credibility of nutrition policy beyond this single document.

To their credit, the new guidelines rightly emphasize the gravity of America’s health burdens. More than half of U.S. adults live with diabetes or prediabetes. Over 40 percent meet the criteria for obesity. Cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of death in America. The guidelines also emphasize reducing consumption of highly processed foods. 

Scientists on the advisory committee prioritized plant-based proteins and seafood over red meat based on long-term evidence linking diets rich in legumes, whole grains, nuts and seafood, and lower in red and processed meat, to better population health outcomes.

The new guidelines suggest that protein foods are largely interchangeable, but the reality is that not all proteins are created equal. Different proteins contain different nutrients, such as fiber, omega-3 fatty acids and saturated fats, and these nutrients influence disease risk in different ways.

Beef, pork, lamb and processed meats are among the largest contributors of saturated fat in the American diet; scaling up on these proteins is likely to translate into greater saturated fat consumption. Even as the report questioned longstanding consensus on saturated fat, it still recommended limiting it to 10 percent of calories.

Placing beans, peas and lentils below animal proteins on the pyramid is problematic, as these foods are also key sources of fiber. While the prior dietary guidelines had suggested that overall Americans consumed enough protein, it had noted that about 85 percent of Americans underconsume beans, peas and lentils. 

Evidence of the protective role of fiber on disease is solid. A large meta-analysis published in The Lancet found that higher fiber intake was associated with a 15 to 30 percent lower risk of heart disease, type 2 diabetes, colorectal cancer and premature death. 

Fiber also improves satiety and diet quality, making it easier to reduce reliance on ultra-processed foods, a stated goal of the new guidelines. Increasing consumption of foods such as legumes and whole grains must be central to dietary guidance if reducing consumption of ultra-processed foods is truly a priority. But legumes and whole grains now occupy a tiny slice at the bottom of the pyramid.

There are environmental implications as well. Although the Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee is prohibited from weighing environmental evidence, meat and dairy production remain among the most resource-intensive components of the food system, requiring far more land, water and energy than plant-based foods. 

While the dietary guidelines are not environmental policy, they shape food demand across federal programs and the broader marketplace. Encouraging greater consumption of meat and dairy without acknowledging these impacts risks undermining both public health goals and long-term food system resilience.

To be clear, the new guidelines did get several things right. The emphasis on whole and minimally processed foods is a welcome shift away from decades of nutrient-focused messaging. Fruits and vegetables are placed prominently in the new food guide pyramid, and the focus on limiting highly processed foods is also a meaningful step forward.

National dietary data show that Americans generally meet protein needs, while falling far short on fiber, legumes, whole grains and omega-3-rich seafood. Elevating meat and dairy moves us further away from solving those problems.

If the goal of the dietary guidelines is to improve public health, they should prioritize the foods Americans consistently underconsume, not simply promote higher protein intake.

Daphene Altema-Johnson is a senior program officer at Johns Hopkins Center for a Livable Future and a registered dietitian. Andrew Thorne-Lyman is an associate professor of International Health at Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health. Patti Anderson is a policy director and researcher at Johns Hopkins Center for a Livable Future.

data.title: Trump says he's working with Democrats to avert government shutdown
data.description:
President Trump, in a rare move, said Thursday he’s working with Democrats on Capitol Hill in a bipartisan manner to avoid an impending government shutdown as the Senate works through appropriations bills being tied up over Department of Homeland Security funding.

“Hopefully, we won’t have a shutdown. We’re working on that right now. I think we’re getting close. The Democrats, I don’t believe want to see it either. So we’ll work in a very bipartisan way, I believe, not to have a shutdown,” Trump said at his Cabinet meeting. 

A source familiar with the discussions told The Hill that Trump and Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer are working together toward a deal to separate DHS funding from five other House-passed appropriations bills in hopes of avoiding a partial government shutdown this weekend. 

Schumer and Senate Democrats are demanding that the pending DHS funding bill be pulled out of the six-bill appropriations package.

Shortly after Trump’s remarks, Senate Democrats and a few Republicans blocked a major government funding package that needs to pass by midnight Saturday to avoid a government shutdown, signaling that negotiations between President Trump and Senate Democrats still have a way to go before reaching a deal.

The Democratic leader wants to pass a short-term extension of funding for the agency to give Congress time to negotiate and pass legislation to overhaul Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Customs and Border Protection (CBP) to “rein in ICE and the violence.”

“This is not border security, this is not law and order, this is chaos — created at the top and felt in so many of our neighborhoods,” Schumer said Wednesday at a press conference.

data.title: Trump says he will announce new Fed Chair 'sometime next week'
data.description:
President Trump revealed on Thursday that he will be announcing his pick to chair the Federal Reserve “sometime next week.”  

“We are going to be announcing the head of the Fed, who that will be,” Trump said at his monthly Cabinet meeting at the White House. “It will be a person that will, I think, do a good job.” 

“We’re paying far too much interest in the Fed. The Fed rate is too high, unacceptably high,” he continued. 

The revelation comes as the president has slammed current Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, whose term is up in May,  for not lowering interest rates. 

“Jerome “Too Late” Powell again refused to cut interest rates, even though he has absolutely no reason to keep them so high. He is hurting our Country, and its National Security,” Trump wrote in a lengthy TruthSocial post earlier on Thursday.

The Fed declined to cut interest rates on Wednesday, opting to hold them steady amid the president’s pressure campaign. 

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) voted 10-2 to keep its baseline interest rate range unchanged at 3.5 percent to 3.75 percent. 

The announcement on interest rates came after the Department of Justice launched a criminal investigation into Powell, which he has strongly pushed back on, including announcing the probe in a pre-recorded video.

National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett and former Fed Board governor Kevin Warsh have been floated as two favorites for the job.
